does: This paper introduces AutoBackdoor, an automated framework that uses language model agents to generate and deploy backdoor attacks against large language models. Rather than relying on manually designed triggers, the framework employs an autonomous agent to create semantically coherent, context-aware trigger phrases that can be inserted into training data across diverse domains. The system automates the entire pipeline from trigger generation through poisoned dataset construction to model fine-tuning, enabling scalable injection of hidden malicious behaviors into LLMs with minimal human intervention. The approach moves beyond static, hand-crafted backdoor methods to demonstrate how modern AI agents can systematically generate adaptive triggers suited to arbitrary topics and contexts.
connects: This work extends the emerging literature on backdoor attacks for LLMs, which has traditionally relied on simple, manually-crafted triggers and fixed poisoning strategies. AutoBackdoor builds on prior research in adversarial attacks on language models and red-teaming frameworks, adapting agent-based autonomy concepts—increasingly studied in AI safety—to the backdoor attack domain. The paper responds to limitations in existing defense evaluation methodologies, which often test against narrow, predetermined threat models rather than diverse, adaptive adversaries. It draws connections between automated threat generation and the broader challenge of comprehensive robustness assessment, positioning itself within conversations about evaluating LLM safety under realistic, scaling adversarial conditions that reflect how actual malicious actors might operate.
enables: By automating backdoor attack generation, this framework significantly enhances red-teaming capabilities for evaluating LLM defenses at scale. It enables researchers and safety practitioners to systematically stress-test models against diverse, adaptive trigger mechanisms rather than just fixed baselines, potentially revealing vulnerabilities that static attacks miss. The work points toward more rigorous benchmarking of backdoor robustness and motivates development of defenses that must contend with adaptive adversaries. Looking forward, AutoBackdoor establishes methodology for how autonomous agents can be leveraged in adversarial evaluation, potentially informing broader automated security testing frameworks for AI systems and highlighting the importance of defensive mechanisms that remain robust against systematically-generated rather than human-enumerated attacks.
